title: Termination-Dependent Surface States and Magnetic Fingerprints of Chiral Helimagnet Cr1/3TaS2
authors: Bo Liang, Xue Li, Congcong Le, Zirui Wu, Wenpei Zhu, Neng Cai, Yong-Chang Lau, Xianxin Wu, Jiayu Liu, Zhanfeng Liu, Hongen Zhu, Tongrui Li, Zhicheng Jiang, Yu Huang, Wenchuan Jing, Xun Ma, Qi Jiang, Hang Li, Zhihao Cai, Xuezhi Chen, Gexing Qu, Yiwei Cheng, Bing-Jie Chen, Zhengtai Liu, Dawei Shen, Mao Ye, Shengtao Cui, Zhe Sun, Koji Miyamoto, Taichi Okuda, Kenya Shimada, Yaobo Huang, Zhenhua Chen, Lin Zhao, Baojie Feng, Xinguo Ren, Wenhong Wang, Xingjiang Zhou, Guodong Liu
abstract: Chiral helimagnets based on intercalated transition-metal dichalcogenides, characterized by nano-scale spin ordering, provide a powerful route to engineer chiral spin textures (e.g. the topologically protected magnetic solitons) and emergent electronic functionality at reduced dimensions, where surface and interface states often dominate device operation. However, despite growing interest, direct experimental studies of termination-dependent surface electronic structures and their temperature-driven magnetic evolution remain largely unexplored, hindering a microscopic understanding of the electronic states that is crucial for the development of low-dimensional spintronic devices. Here, for the first time, taking Cr1/3TaS2 as a representative example, we systematically investigate the termination-dependent surface electronic states of the chiral helimagnets and uncover their distinct temperature evolution across the magnetic transition (TC~142K) by combining high-resolution ARPES with a micro-focused beam and surface-state-resolved first-principles calculations. The TaS2-terminated surface hosts folded monolayer-like TaS2 bands under the $\sqrt3\times\sqrt3$ superlattice potential and a shallow triangular electron pocket at the superlattice $\bar K$ point arising from Cr-Ta orbital hybridization. In contrast, the Cr-terminated surface exhibits reconstructed hole pockets with pronounced magnetic band splitting. This splitting disappears above TC and closely follows the chiral helimagnetic order parameter, providing a direct spectroscopic fingerprint of chiral helimagnetic order. In addition, multiple ultranarrow Cr-d-derived surface flat bands are resolved. These findings establish Cr1/3TaS2 as a model system in which surface electronic states are strongly coupled to chiral magnetism, opening new opportunities for chiral spintronic and valleytronic micro/nanodevices.
summary: This paper provides the first systematic investigation of how surface termination affects the electronic and magnetic properties of the chiral helimagnet Cr1/3TaS2. By using micro-ARPES, the authors show that the Cr-terminated surface provides a direct spectroscopic fingerprint of the chiral magnetic order through temperature-dependent band splitting. These findings are crucial for designing future chiral spintronic and valleytronic devices.
main_problem: The study aims to resolve the lack of microscopic understanding regarding how surface termination and temperature-driven magnetic evolution affect the electronic structures in the chiral helimagnet Cr1/3TaS2.
main_result: The researchers discovered that the Cr-terminated surface exhibits pronounced magnetic band splitting that tracks the chiral helimagnetic order parameter, while the TaS2-terminated surface shows different electronic features like a triangular electron pocket.
method: The study combines high-resolution micro-focused ARPES with surface-state-resolved first-principles DFT calculations and various magnetic characterization techniques like Lorentz-TEM and magnetometry.
model_or_system: The physical system is Cr1/3TaS2, a chiral helimagnet based on intercalated transition-metal dichalcogenides featuring a sqrt3xsqrt3 superlattice and nano-scale spin textures.
key_observables: Surface electronic states, magnetic band splitting, temperature-dependent magnetization (M-T), and Cr-d-derived surface flat bands.
important_parameters: Magnetic transition temperature (TC ~ 142 K), surface termination (TaS2 vs Cr), and in-plane magnetic field strength.
main_assumptions: The theoretical modeling uses an effective ferromagnetic approximation rather than the full chiral helimagnetic state due to supercell size constraints.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows crystal structure and magnetic properties; Figure 2 shows field-dependent magnetization and Lorentz-TEM images; Figure 3 shows Brillouin zone and calculated band structures; Figures 6 and 7 demonstrate the temperature evolution of electronic bands and magnetic splitting on different surface terminations.
paper_structure: The paper begins with structural and magnetic characterization, moves to the investigation of bulk and surface electronic structures using ARPES and DFT, explores the temperature-dependent evolution of these states across the magnetic transition, and concludes with the implications for spintronic applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Crystal structure and magnetic properties of Cr1/3TaS2. (a) Crystal structure of Cr1/3TaS2, where Cr atoms are intercalated between adjacent TaS2 layers. (b) Top view of the Cr1/3TaS2 lattice along the c-axis, illustrating the in-plane ordering of intercalated Cr atoms. (c) X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of a high-quality Cr1/3TaS2 sample. (d) EDX spectrum of a Cr1/3TaS2 single crystal, showing characteristic peaks from Ta, S, and Cr. (e) Temperature dependence of the longitudinal resistivity ρxx measured at zero magnetic field, with a residual resistivity ratio (RRR) of 4.46. (f) Temperature-dependent magnetization measured under zero-field-cooling (ZFC) and field-cooling (FC)...

Figure 2. Field-induced evolution of magnetic properties in Cr1/3TaS2. (a) Magnetic field dependence of the magnetization (M-H) measured with the magnetic field applied within the ab plane (H || ab) at 5, 60, 120, and 180 K. (b) M-H curves measured with the magnetic field applied along the crystallographic c axis (H || c) at the same temperatures in (a). (c) Temperature dependence of the Hall resistivity ρxy as a function of magnetic field (H || c) from 5–180 K for Cr1/3TaS2 single crystal. (d) Lorentz-TEM images acquired at 92 K, showing the continuous evolution of chiral helical magnetic structures under in-plane magnetic field ranging from 0 T to 1.13 T.
